author: @DeanneMariaCreates
text: Hi Robert, Now that you have used it, would you show how to calibrate it please?

author: @RoadRashSpirit
text: I bought one of these many years ago, its more or less a table now. Unlike many in the comments I found this to be woefully inacurate because theres too much flex in the gate. Was ok for doing small lengths. Overall wish Id spent more, I do like evolution stuff generally but I may infuture make my own table and use the inards.

author: @robinfielding9506
text: I need something like that cause strate cuts are not me

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: same here mate lol

author: @royharkins7066
text:
Oops I forgot too ask Rob what bore is that evaluation blade 
Cheers

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: what do you mean by bore mate - do you mean centre hole? if so it is a standard size I believe

author: @royharkins7066
text: I spent over 2k renewing all my 18v gear, bought  Makita which I thought was the best , maybe it was till they started making it in China ,where they use cheaper parts, hence my 18v Makita circular saw caught fire after less than 2 hours use !! Chinesium & chitanium!! I’m well hacked off I’ve lost confidence in Makita I think their all at it ……buyer beware 😑

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: like you I did like Makita but I had a Hitachi cordless I used on site continuously for 2 years - so really heavy use and it did an amazing job - it was a little more expensive but to be honest an awesome tool

author: @grafton261
text: I have one of these under a different brand name , we used it among other things to rip 100+ year old Australian floor boards to re use them in my house renovations ( the tongue & groove edges had rotted over the decades ) , the saw performed very well indeed especially since it was cheap at $200AU , it even came with 3 spare Carbide tipped blades

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: these lower priced tools can be surprisingly good

author: @spanishflew
text: watch out for kick back

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice tip mate - cheers

author: @badWithComputer
text: I have one of these, throw the mitre slide away firstly, then make a sled for it, it will improve the accuracy.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice one mate thanks for the advice

author: @denisstump5874
text: I heard you could aim three lasers at each other in a vacuum tube or chamber and create almost absolute zero

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: interesting - cheers

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

author: @maryhinge128
text: Nah, hand tools only.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - I get the purity - but to be honest it would have taken ages to cut 120 strips by hand - though I suppose I could have made a cutting jig - cheers

author: @rayg436
text: I absolutely agree with you buy the tool for the intended job or you may not be happy with the finished product otherwise.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

author: @totherarf
text:
Definition of Instruction Manual ........ The manufacturers Opinion of how you should operate a piece of kit!



Handy as a backup though! ;o)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: personally I like to discover what a thing can do! but yeah handy to have around if you get stumped

author: @andymilligan8485
text: Please don't use gloves around a table saw.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I hate splinters!

author: @stevetobias4890
text: I have a table saw and many other woodworking tools. I have made up a few sleds and other adaptors for the table saw to make specific cuts and joints etc.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: fancy sharing a few mate? The saw is new to me and I could do with a little 'expert' advice

replies.author: @stevetobias4890
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering sure, firstly make sure that you have some pieces of wood that fit in the slots that are parallel to the blade. Then you can glue and screw them to a piece of plywood and then make sure that the wood backing is square but thick enough to only cut halfway through. I will send you some photos. There is also another for finger joints

author: @kelvinsparks4651
text: I like the evolution brand also, I have the hand circular saw and the chop saw though my table saw is another brand of the same price.  The best advice i can give is couple it to a shop vac or you will soon be swimming in dust lol

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - nice advice and couldn't agree more

author: @hunter00047
text: I have had one of these for at least 7 years and it's great. The only thing that will fail are the legs as they are too thin. The welder and some old conduit tube sorted that. It has become one of my must have tools. 👍👍😁

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice tip mate - I will get welding!

author: @rhysstatham7407
text: Seeing as you have a large workbench you could build that saw into it.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: like it! - that's a really good idea mate - cheers

author: @JustinMurray170fin
text: Do yourself a favour and don't use gloves around such rotary machines, it could be the different between losing a digit or your whole hand. If the glove gets caught it'll pull your hand in.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate - though I do hate getting splinters- it's good advice

author: @carstenweiland7896
text: Loved my saw, please take care about the kickback effect these saws can generate. It is the tool with the highest amount of work accidents at all, forget chainsaws they are safer.

replies.author: @carstenweiland7896
replies.text: @Gazr Gazr All I said is that if you look at statistics chainsaws are safe!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it is something to respect that's for sure

author: @karlmyers6518
text: I recently bought a evolution chop saw because I've had the table saw for roughly 5 years. It's a good table saw for the money but what my chippy has shown me. He uses a plunge saw with rails for most stuff so that's my next purchase.  After my cordless sander

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you sound like you have an awesome shop mate

replies.author: @karlmyers6518
replies.text: @Robert Murray-Smith  It's getting there mate.
